We have modelled D. melanogaster and fission yeast, and the models are equivalent with very minor differences (eg. MIM complex in yeast is replaced by a functionally analogous system in metazoa). This is the set of terms required to describe all mitochondrial import pathways: - [x] 1. GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport) Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol to their final destination in the mitochondria. - [ ] 2. GO_NEW ~mitochondrial outer membrane alpha helical protein insertion~ (1. In the image) alpha helical protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane Narrow synonym MIM pathway (different complex in higher eukaryotes) Narrow synonym: MTCH pathway Definition: The import of alpha helical and tail-anchored proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial outer membrane. Note:In yeast, the insertase is the mitochondrial import (MIM) complex, composed of Mim1 and Mim2. This reaction is ROS-sensitive -> mitochondrial oxidative stress directly increases IMS recruitment of both CCS1 and SOD1. Metalation and oxidative folding of Sod1: Sod1 requires copper and a zinc ion cofactors. Ccs1 directly mediates copper delivery to Sod1 and forms a transient intermolecular disulfide with Sod1 to facilitate copper transfer and disulfide bond formation simultaneously. CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.
